## Introduction

The convergence of global population aging and therapeutic advances in oncology has produced a historically unprecedented challenge: a rapidly expanding cohort of cancer patients who survive long enough to require sustained, structured long-term care. Globally, cancer remains one of the leading causes of morbidity and mortality, yet the prognosis for many cancers has improved dramatically over recent decades. In Canada alone, cancer is the primary cause of mortality, responsible for 30.2% of all deaths, while simultaneously the number of individuals living beyond an initial cancer diagnosis continues to grow year over year as prevention strategies and treatment protocols improve [@Dor2022]. In Japan, where the proportion of the population aged 65 years or older reached 25% in 2013 and is projected to exceed 39.9% by 2060, cancer and other age-associated diseases have made the transition from acute curative medicine to sustained, community-oriented care an urgent national priority [@Arai2015]. The United States faces a parallel trajectory: the number of long-term cancer survivors is expected to double by 2050 relative to current figures, placing enormous pressure on a primary care infrastructure that has historically been ill-equipped to manage this population [@Bober2009]. This intersection of demographic aging and oncological survivorship is not merely a quantitative challenge; it fundamentally redefines what "cancer care" means across the full disease trajectory, from diagnosis and active treatment through recovery, remission surveillance, and, for many older adults, end-of-life management within institutional care settings.

Long-term care (LTC) for cancer patients encompasses a broad and heterogeneous spectrum of needs, settings, and providers. It includes follow-up surveillance for recurrence, management of treatment-related late effects, psychosocial support, palliative and end-of-life care, and the coordination of services across specialist oncology teams, general practitioners, community nursing, and residential care facilities. The concept of cancer as a chronic disease requiring sustained management rather than discrete curative episodes is now well established [@Linden2016], yet its practical implementation remains inconsistent across care systems. Skin cancer, for instance, is the most frequently diagnosed malignancy among older adults residing in nursing homes, with prevalence estimates ranging from 15% to 40% across residential care studies, yet access to specialized dermatologic care within these facilities is often severely limited, and standardized screening protocols remain absent [@Garelick2026]. Gibson and Gorman similarly documented that cancer diagnoses are present in approximately 15% of LTC facility residents, a population whose care needs intersect oncological complexity with the frailty, multimorbidity, and functional dependence characteristic of LTC settings [@Gibson2012]. In Japan, community-based integrated care models have sought to bridge designated cancer hospitals and local nursing care facilities, but qualitative investigations involving 88 healthcare and long-term care providers identified seven distinct barriers to effective collaboration, including insufficient sharing of patients' illness perspectives and inadequate coordination between cancer-specific and non-cancer symptom management, with older cancer patients disproportionately affected by fragmented care [@Mashiro2023]. These structural failures underscore the systemic gap between the aspirational ideal of integrated cancer LTC and its lived reality across diverse healthcare contexts.

The Knowledge, Attitudes, and Beliefs (KAB) framework—also rendered as Knowledge, Attitudes, and Practices (KAP) in adjacent health behaviour literature—offers a theoretically grounded lens through which to analyze why gaps between available knowledge and actual care behaviour persist among both healthcare professionals and patients. Originally developed in public health to understand determinants of health-related behaviour change, the KAB framework posits that adequate knowledge is a necessary but insufficient condition for appropriate behaviour: attitudes and beliefs mediate the translation of knowledge into practice. In oncology survivorship and LTC, this tripartite framework is particularly illuminating because deficits may arise at each level independently and interact in complex ways. Nurses caring for haematological cancer survivors in an Australian tertiary centre, for instance, reported that while they broadly agreed survivorship interventions fell within their professional scope, they identified significant deficits in confidence—a direct attitudinal and knowledge construct—specifically around discussing fertility, sexuality, and psychosocial distress screening, with lack of time and educational resources named as the primary structural barriers [@Wallace2015]. A nationally representative survey of 1,130 US oncologists and 1,020 primary care physicians found that fewer than 5% of oncologists consistently provided written survivorship care plans, and only 12% of primary care physicians regularly discussed survivorship care recommendations with their patients; oncologists who had received formal training in late and long-term effects were more than twice as likely to initiate such discussions (OR 2.02; 95% CI, 1.51–2.70), confirming that knowledge acquisition directly and measurably shapes clinical behaviour [@BlanchHartigan2014]. The KAB framework therefore provides a structured vocabulary for diagnosing where in the knowledge-to-behaviour pipeline care quality deteriorates.

The gap between professional knowledge and patient-centred long-term care needs is perhaps most starkly documented in studies of information provision during clinical follow-up. Mellblom and colleagues video-recorded consultations between 10 Norwegian pediatric oncologists and 66 adolescent survivors of leukemia or lymphoma (ages 12–20) and found that while potential late effects were raised in 85% of consultations, extended information was provided in only 29% of cases, with substantial variability attributable to individual physician practice rather than patient characteristics such as age, time since treatment, or late-effect risk profile [@Mellblom2015]. This physician-level variability in knowledge-sharing behaviour illustrates precisely the attitudinal and practice dimensions that KAB analyses must interrogate. In primary care settings, Arreskov and colleagues analyzed video recordings of 14 general practitioner consultations with patients who had a history of cancer and found that most GPs exited patients' voiced concerns by interrupting, superficially acknowledging, or failing to explore emotional dimensions, attending instead to the fixed consultation agenda—a pattern inconsistent with the comprehensive needs of cancer survivors in long-term follow-up [@Arreskov2022]. The consequences of such communication failures extend beyond individual clinical encounters. Young adult survivors of childhood cancer participating in a Delphi consensus process identified a lack of knowledge on the part of both physicians and survivors regarding long-term health sequelae as the primary barrier to appropriate follow-up care, calling explicitly for self-advocacy training for survivors and advanced post-graduate training for primary care providers [@Zebrack2004]. These findings converge on a recurring pattern: professional knowledge deficits, attitudinal discomfort, and structural disincentives combine to produce a systematic underdelivery of long-term cancer care across multiple countries and healthcare systems.

Survivor-side KAB deficits are equally consequential. Patients who receive inadequate information about late effects cannot engage in effective self-management, informed shared decision-making, or appropriate symptom monitoring [@Heathcote2018]. In blood and marrow transplant (BMT) recipients recruited across England, qualitative analysis identified recurrent themes of uncertainty and information insufficiency during the transition from acute transplant care to long-term follow-up monitoring, with patients expressing that "there was not much information about what to expect or be aware of" [@Bell2024]. BMT survivors in New South Wales, Australia, similarly expressed concerns about the capacity of community primary care providers to manage complex post-transplant needs, particularly in rural and regional areas, while simultaneously recognising that reliance on specialist tertiary centres indefinitely is unsustainable [@McErlean2026]. The Children's Oncology Group (COG), surveying 179 of 220 affiliated institutions across the United States, found that the most prevalent barrier to effective survivorship care delivery was a perceived insufficient knowledge on the part of clinicians receiving transition referrals—a finding with direct implications for the structural organisation of LTC for cancer patients transitioning between care levels [@EshelmanKent2011]. In Germany, the OPTILATER protocol, designed to survey up to 3,300 long-term cancer survivors across multiple cancer types, was initiated precisely because existing healthcare systems were judged insufficiently adapted to meet the late physical, emotional, and socio-economic sequelae of treatment, with particular recognition that vulnerable subgroups—including those with migration backgrounds—face compounded barriers to care access [@Martin2025].

Geriatric assessment (GA) represents one domain where the KAB gap among oncology professionals has attracted sustained intervention-level scrutiny. In Japan, GA remains underused despite its conceptual alignment with the existing Long-Term Care Insurance (LTCI) system; workforce limitations, structural barriers, and low professional awareness of its clinical utility have collectively prevented uptake, even as randomised evidence demonstrates that active multidisciplinary GA implementation improves treatment toxicity profiles, functional status, communication quality, and quality of life in older cancer patients [@Yamamoto2026]. The focus on life-prolonging anticancer treatment has similarly been identified as a structural attitude that impedes shared decision-making in people with advanced cancer, effectively silencing patient values and preferences when they diverge from curative treatment goals [@DJM2024]. Advanced cancer care planning, fertility preservation counselling, and palliative care integration all represent domains where professional attitudes—not merely knowledge deficits—determine whether patients receive care concordant with their actual needs and preferences [@V2021; @RL2025].

Across these converging literatures, a systematic review synthesizing KAB-related data from cancer patients and healthcare professionals within LTC settings has not previously been conducted. Although narrative reviews have addressed specific tumour types, care transitions, or individual professional groups, no study has mapped the full scope of knowledge, attitude, and practice constructs across the cancer-LTC interface in an integrated, PRISMA-compliant framework. This gap is consequential: without a structured evidence synthesis, clinicians, educators, and policymakers lack the foundation required to design targeted, theoretically grounded interventions to close the professional knowledge-to-practice gap and strengthen patient self-management capacity.

This systematic review therefore pursues three interrelated objectives. First, to identify and characterise the domains of knowledge, attitudes, and practices among healthcare professionals involved in the long-term care of cancer patients, including oncologists, general practitioners, specialist nurses, and long-term care facility staff. Second, to describe the corresponding KAB profiles of cancer patients and their informal caregivers within LTC contexts, with particular attention to unmet informational needs, attitudinal barriers to care engagement, and behaviour patterns affecting health outcomes. Third, to identify determinants—individual, interpersonal, organisational, and systemic—that predict KAB gaps and their consequences for quality of cancer long-term care, in order to inform the development of evidence-based educational and structural interventions. By bringing together and synthesizing primary data from multiple care settings and professional contexts under a unified KAB framework, this review aims to establish a rigorous evidence base for improving the quality and continuity of long-term cancer care in aging populations worldwide.
